project_help: Selma is an interactive tool for visualizing and exploring hierarchical taxonomies. Use the toolbar to change view modes, search for specific nodes, or export the diagram.

era_3000bc.name: 3000 BC
era_3000bc.description: The dawn of writing in Mesopotamia and Egypt.

era_2000bc.name: 2000 BC
era_2000bc.description: The first alphabets and cursive syllabaries.

era_1000bc.name: 1000 BC
era_1000bc.description: The Iron Age and the explosion of alphabetic scripts.

era_0bc.name: Late Antiquity
era_0bc.description: Around the turn of the era.

era_500ad.name: Early Middle Ages
era_500ad.description: Emergence of scripts linked to major religions.

writing_systems_root.name: Writing Systems
writing_systems_root.description: The major writing systems of the world and their genealogical relationships.

egyptian_hieroglyphs.name: Egyptian Hieroglyphs
egyptian_hieroglyphs.description: One of the earliest writing systems, combining logographic, syllabic and alphabetic elements. Active c. 3200 BCE–394 CE.

hieratic.name: Hieratic
hieratic.description: Cursive priestly script derived from hieroglyphs, used on papyrus from c. 2600 BCE.

demotic.name: Demotic
demotic.description: Simplified administrative script of ancient Egypt, used c. 650 BCE–5th century CE.

proto_sinaitic.name: Proto-Sinaitic
proto_sinaitic.description: The earliest alphabetic script, c. 1850–1400 BCE; ancestor of nearly all modern alphabets.

sumerian_cuneiform.name: Sumerian Cuneiform
sumerian_cuneiform.description: The oldest attested writing system in the world, c. 3100 BCE; logosyllabic, written on clay tablets.

akkadian.name: Akkadian Cuneiform
akkadian.description: Semitic adaptation of Sumerian cuneiform; lingua franca of the ancient Near East c. 1500–600 BCE. Now extinct.

ugaritic.name: Ugaritic
ugaritic.description: Unique DAG script: an alphabetic cuneiform combining the Semitic alphabet with the cuneiform medium. c. 1400–1185 BCE.

phoenician.name: Phoenician
phoenician.description: 22-letter abjad, c. 1050 BCE; the most influential alphabet in history, ancestor of Greek, Arabic, Hebrew and many more.

aramaic.name: Aramaic
aramaic.description: Administrative language of the Persian Empire; parent of Hebrew, Nabataean, Syriac, Sogdian, and Brahmi scripts.

hebrew.name: Hebrew
hebrew.description: 22-letter abjad, DAG node (Phoenician + Aramaic); sacred script of Judaism, revived as a spoken language in the 20th century.

nabataean.name: Nabataean
nabataean.description: Script of the Nabataean Kingdom (Petra); direct ancestor of the Arabic script. c. 200 BCE–4th century CE.

syriac.name: Syriac
syriac.description: Literary language of Eastern Christianity; contributed to Arabic and Armenian scripts. Still used liturgically.

arabic.name: Arabic
arabic.description: DAG node (Nabataean + Syriac); 28-letter fully cursive abjad; second most widely used writing system with ~1.8 billion users.

sogdian.name: Sogdian
sogdian.description: Script of the Silk Road merchants; intermediary that passed the Aramaic tradition to Central and East Asia.

uyghur.name: Old Uyghur
uyghur.description: Vertical script of the Uyghur Khaganate, c. 8th century CE; direct model for the Mongolian script.

mongolian.name: Mongolian
mongolian.description: Top-to-bottom vertical script created c. 1204 CE; still official in Inner Mongolia (China). Undergoing revival in Mongolia.

south_arabian.name: South Arabian
south_arabian.description: Script of the ancient Arabian kingdoms (Saba, Qataban, Himyar); ancestor of the Ge'ez script. c. 900 BCE–600 CE.

geez.name: Ge'ez (Ethiopic)
geez.description: First abugida in history; descended from South Arabian. Used today for Amharic, Tigrinya and other Ethiopian/Eritrean languages.

brahmi.name: Brahmi
brahmi.description: Foundational script of South Asia, c. 3rd century BCE; ancestor of over 100 modern scripts including Devanagari, Thai and Tibetan.

devanagari.name: Devanagari
devanagari.description: Abugida script with distinctive top bar; used for Hindi, Sanskrit, Marathi and Nepali. ~600 million users.

tibetan.name: Tibetan
tibetan.description: Script created in the 7th century CE to translate Buddhist scriptures; parent of Phags-pa. Used for Tibetan and Dzongkha.

phags_pa.name: Phags-pa
phags_pa.description: Universal script of the Mongol Empire, 1269 CE; derived from Tibetan, written vertically. Possible influence on Hangul.

greek.name: Greek
greek.description: First true alphabet with both consonants and vowels, c. 800 BCE; ancestor of Latin, Cyrillic, Coptic, Armenian and Glagolitic.

coptic.name: Coptic
coptic.description: DAG node (Greek + Demotic); final stage of the Egyptian language; liturgical script of the Coptic Orthodox Church.

etruscan.name: Etruscan
etruscan.description: Script of the Etruscan civilization, c. 700 BCE; direct structural model for the Latin alphabet.

latin.name: Latin
latin.description: DAG node (Etruscan + Greek); c. 700 BCE; parent of the Modern Roman alphabet and all Romance languages' writing systems.

modern_roman.name: Modern Roman Alphabet
modern_roman.description: The world's most widely used writing system, used by ~3 billion people across 100+ languages.

glagolitic.name: Glagolitic
glagolitic.description: Oldest Slavic script, created 862–863 CE by Saints Cyril and Methodius; parent of Cyrillic. Survived in Croatia until the 19th century.

cyrillic.name: Cyrillic
cyrillic.description: DAG node (Greek + Glagolitic); 9th–10th century CE; used by Russian, Bulgarian, Serbian and ~250 million people.

armenian.name: Armenian
armenian.description: DAG node (Greek + Syriac); created in 405 CE by Mesrop Mashtots; unchanged for 1,600 years.

georgian.name: Georgian
georgian.description: Distinctive script with three historical forms (Asomtavruli, Nuskhuri, Mkhedruli); official script of Georgia.

oracle_bones.name: Oracle Bone Script
oracle_bones.description: Earliest Chinese writing, c. 1200 BCE (Shang dynasty); inscribed on turtle shells for divination.

classical_chinese.name: Classical Chinese
classical_chinese.description: Logographic writing system evolved from Oracle Bone Script; literary lingua franca of East Asia for 2,000 years.

kanji.name: Kanji (Japanese)
kanji.description: Chinese characters adopted in Japan, 4th–5th century CE; one of three scripts used simultaneously in modern Japanese.

hiragana_katakana.name: Hiragana / Katakana
hiragana_katakana.description: Two syllabic scripts derived from Kanji in the 9th century CE; together with Kanji and Rōmaji, form the Japanese writing system.

hanja.name: Hanja (Korean)
hanja.description: Chinese characters as used in Korean, from c. 2nd century CE; parent of Hangul. Still used for disambiguation in South Korea.

hangul.name: Hangul (Korean)
hangul.description: DAG node (Hanja + Phags-pa); created 1443 CE; considered one of the most scientifically designed alphabets ever devised.
